### Post by psyguy92 on 2005-11-11
Filariasis

(Note: If you don't know and don't want to google it, here's the definition:  
     (Med.) a disease caused by nematodes in the blood or
      tissues of the body causing blockage of lymphatic vessels.
      Death of the adult worms may lead to inflammation and
      permanent fibrosis; the condition may lead to serious
      symptoms, such as elephantiasis or blindness. See river
      blindness. )
I thought that was a better association than my first thought :>

### Post by Mr Wrath on 2006-03-08
HIPPOPOTO*MONSTRO*SESQUIPED*AL*IAN
or lets try...
TAUMATA*WHAKA*TANGI*HANGA*KOAUAU*O*TAMATEA*TURIPUKAKA*PIKI*MAUNGA*HORO*NUKU*POKAI*WHENUA*KITANA*TAHU

A hill in New Zealand. This Maori name was in general use, but is now generally abbreviated to Taumata. The name means: the summit of the hill, where Tamatea, who is known as the land eater, slid down, climbed up and swallowed mountains, played on his nose flute to his loved one.
Lets get the words shorter...

catlett: looks like two Dung Beatles to me.

P.S. yes that is the explanation of the artwork by M.C. Escher. :p

honestly Escher was Dutch so Scarabs may be a mis-translation or perhaps  Dung Beatles have a similar tendency to roll s***?

EDIT: actually a Scarab is a Dung Beatle:

**Scarabaeidae**


The family **Scarabaeidae** consists of over 30,000 species of [beetles]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beetle") worldwide. The species in this large family are often called **scarabs** or **scarab beetles**.
 Scarabs are stout-bodied beetles, many with bright metallic colors, measuring between 5-60 mm. They have distinctive, clubbed [antennae]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antennae") composed of plates called [lamellae]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lamellae") that can be compressed into a ball or fanned out like leaves to sense odors. The front legs are broad and adapted for digging.
 The C-shaped [larvae]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Larvae"), called grubs, are pale yellow or white. Most adult beetles are nocturnal, although the flower chafers ([Cetoniinae]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cetoniinae")) and many leaf chafers ([Rutelinae]("http://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Rutelinae&action=edit")) are active during the day. The grubs mostly live underground or under debris, so are not exposed to sunlight. Many Scarabs are scavengers that recycle dung, [carrion]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carrion"), or decaying plant material. Others, such as the [Japanese Beetle]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Japanese_Beetle") are devastating agricultural pests.
 Some of the well-known beetles from the Scarabaeidae are Japanese Beetles, [Dung beetles]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dung_beetle"), [June beetles]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/June_beetle"), [Rose chafers]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rose_chafer"), [Rhinoceros beetles]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rhinoceros_beetle"), [Hercules beetles]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hercules_beetle") and [Goliath beetles]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Goliath_beetle").

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scarabaeidae]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scarabaeidae")

Also see this page:

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scarab](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scarab)

A **scarab** or **scarab beetle** may refer to:
 [LIST]
[*]A beetle which belong to the family [Scarabaeidae]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scarabaeidae").
[*]A [dung beetle]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dung_beetle"), especially the *Scarabaeus sacer* worshipped by the [ancient Egyptians]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ancient_Egypt") (an [amulet]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amulet") made by that people in the shape of the species is also called a *scarab*).[/LIST]

Perhaps the next version after eft could be Ubuntu __________ Scarab...

### Post by Biltong (Dee) on 2006-06-16
**Open Source**

[QUOTE=bruce89]Burning ROM (a bit odd that name, i've always thought)[/QUOTE]

It's Nero Burning Rome (Just missing the E)

During the night of July 18, 64 AD, fire broke out in the merchant area of the city of Rome. Fanned by summer winds, the flames quickly spread through the dry, wooden structures of the Imperial City. Soon the fire took on a life of its own consuming all in its path for six days and seven nights. When the conflagration finally ran its course it left seventy percent of the city in smoldering ruins.
Rumors soon arose accusing the Emperor Nero of ordering the torching of the city and standing on the summit of the Palatine playing his lyre as flames devoured the world around him. These rumors have never been confirmed.
[http://www.eyewitnesstohistory.com/rome.htm](http://www.eyewitnesstohistory.com/rome.htm)

### Post by Biltong (Dee) on 2006-06-27

Oops - looked it up wrong :-)

**Should I be concerned about Dihydrogen Monoxide?**

Yes, you should be concerned about DHMO! Although the U.S. Government and the Centers for Disease Control (CDC) do not classify Dihydrogen Monoxide as a toxic or carcinogenic substance (as it does with better known chemicals such as hydrochloric acid and benzene), DHMO is a constituent of many known toxic substances, diseases and disease-causing agents, environmental hazards and can even be lethal to humans in quantities as small as a thimbleful.

[http://www.dhmo.org/facts.html](http://www.dhmo.org/facts.html)

### Post by BWF89 on 2006-07-14
short story

(we read this short story in english class last year. It was about these 2 families that lives on opposite sides of the woods. There was a part of the woods both families layed claim to. One day a man from one family encountered a man from the other family. They were about to kill eachother when lightning struck a tree and pinned them both under it. As the hours passed they got to talking and realized that all the fighting was stupid and they were going to try to work things out. They saw something coming in the distance and thought it was people coming to see where they are and to rescue them. But as they got closer they realized it was a **pack of wolves**. I guess the moral of the story is that if you dont work out your problems now there might not be a later. Anyone know what this story is called? I'd like to read it again)

### Post by RAV TUX on 2006-07-22
[**Otto** **Schott**]("http://msxml.webcrawler.com/info.wbcrwl/clickit/search?r_aid=08C9208E0CDB4A16925BAD4820E1D740&r_eop=2&r_sacop=15&r_spf=0&r_cop=main-title&r_snpp=9&r_spp=6&qqn=pEP.CVqj&r_coid=239138&rawto=http://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Otto_Schott")

> A key figure and one of the forefathers of modern glass research              was the German scientist Otto Schott (1851-1935), who used scientific              methods to study the effects of numerous chemical elements on the              optical and thermal properties of glass. In the field of optical glass,              Schott teamed up with Ernst Abbe (1840-1905), a professor at the University              of Jena and joint owner of the Carl Zeiss firm, to make significant              technological advances 
little known fact Otto was also the inventor of the "Schott" glass, it was actually a high minus optical lens, which they could find no better use of then to fill with alcohol and have "Schotts"

"For a while advertisers used Smurfs to promote Renault, National Benzole, and BP garages and—in the United Kingdom, South Africa, Australia, and New Zealand at least—the figurines were given away when petrol (gasoline) was purchased."

### Post by reacocard on 2006-09-30
proof


This is a interesting little proof a friend of mine worked out.

Women need time and money

Women = Time * Money

Time is money (Time = Money) Therefore,

Women = Money^2

Money is the root of all problems (money = sqrt(problems)) therefore,

Women = Problems

### Post by fuscia on 2006-12-14
is 'swale' a word?

---

### Post by yabbadabbadont on 2006-12-14
> **fuscia said:**
> is 'swale' a word?

wales

Yes, swale is a word:
> 1.	a low place in a tract of land, usually moister and often having ranker vegetation than the adjacent higher land.
2.	a valleylike intersection of two slopes in a piece of land.

time is money
time = money

money is the root of all problems
sqrt(money) = problems

thus,
women = problems[/QUOTE]

> All that is gold does not glitter
Not all who wander are lost

### Post by RAV TUX on 2007-06-11
For those bored with the usual here is another forum word game:

rules are simple make a new word starting with two sequential letters in the middle of the word

This requires that a word be at least four letters.

 You can use any sequence of two letters as long as they are not the first or last letter, keep it in the middle path. ;)

here are examples:

W[COLOR="Red"]or[/COLOR]d>[COLOR="Red"]or[/COLOR][COLOR="SeaGreen"]de[/COLOR]al>[COLOR="SeaGreen"]de[/COLOR][COLOR="Purple"]vi[/COLOR]ate>[COLOR="Purple"]vi[/COLOR]agara>etc, etc.....

The first word is:

**[COLOR="Black"]Syndrome[/COLOR]**

### Post by djchandler on 2007-07-21
round

comment: I'm seeing as much dissociation as association. What is the association from mesa, a Spanish word for flat land at the top of an elevation, and car? I must be dense, because I don't get that at all. Jeep I might understand as being relevant.

I know this is just a silly game, and I suppose I can see mesa>car as being the point, it's just a silly game, but I propose a new rule: that you quote the word you are associating your new word with.

Make sure you are in real time sequence. Of course anyone can break the rule at any time, and essentially begin a whole new sequence.

:lolflag:

---

comment: I'm seeing as much dissociation as association. What is the association from mesa, a Spanish word for flat land at the top of an elevation, and car? I must be dense, because I don't get that at all. Jeep I might understand as being relevant.

I know this is just a silly game, and I suppose I can see mesa>car as being the point, it's just a silly game, but I propose a new rule: that you quote the word you are associating your new word with.

Make sure you are in real time sequence. Of course anyone can break the rule at any time, and essentially begin a whole new sequence.

:lolflag:

Moon.

[comment back : I write whatever word comes to mind when I see the word. Thereby, I associate this word with another. Perhaps someone read Mesa, thought of a car. It's mind-association, not literal. (or I've been playing this game wrong :lolflag:)]

---

### Post by djchandler on 2007-07-27
> **yabbadabbadont said:**
> Ice (house)

Sawdust

Explanation: We used to get ice while camping in Minnesota from an ice house. The ice blocks were cut off of Lake Itasca during the winter, and layers of sawdust were placed between the layers of ice blocks. They had no problems with sufficient ice supply in August when we usually went there. BTW, The Mississippi River starts from the outflow of Lake Itasca, otherwise known as the headwaters of the Mississippi.

@RedSquirrel: Did you have a sizing issue with them?  I just Alt-Right-Click and drag in Fluxbox to force the dialog to resize.  That's a nice feature in Fluxbox, being able to resize dialogs that don't normally allow it.

### Post by xyz on 2008-02-26
implants



> 
	

 
Musical breast implants

Computer chips that store music could soon be built into a woman's breast implants.

One boob could hold an MP3 player and the other the person's whole music collection.

BT futurology, who have developed the idea, say it could be available within 15 years.

BT Laboratories' analyst Ian Pearson said flexible plastic electronics would sit inside the breast. A signal would be relayed to headphones, while the device would be controlled by Bluetooth using a panel on the wrist.

According to The Sun he said: "It is now very hard for me to thing of breast implants as just decorative. If a woman has something implanted permanently, it might as well do something useful."

The sensors around the body linked through the electrical impulses in the chips may also be able to warn wearers about heart murmurs, blood pressure increases, diabetes and breast cancer.

DAILY RFID,a fast growing company with unique expertise in RFID design,manufacturing and engineering, 
has developed UHF Shell Tag-o1, the passive RFID tag with very high performance being used 
on-nonmetal surface,which is especially suitable for tracking applications,such as wooden tracking.

To find out more about these innovate designs and our other unique Shell tags visit us at [http://www.rfid-in-china.com/list.asp?id=233](http://www.rfid-in-china.com/list.asp?id=233)


About DAILY RFID Co.,Ltd.

DAILY RFID Co.,Ltd, which belongs to PAN Group Co., ltd, is the leading company focusing on the research and development of EPC & RFID technology in China. 

We specialize in producing arguably the world's most extensive line of RFID Tag,RFID Label,Smart Card and RFID Reader, which are suitable for any vertical markets.We have obtained the National Integrated Circuit Card Register Certificate, IC Card Manufacture License and ISO9001 Quality Management System Certification.

DAILY's products are designed and manufactured with recognized industry standards relevant to RFID and its markets but most notably for use in the demanding environments to recognize and understand your business's RFID needs. We are commited to providing "Innovative Technology", " Superior, Cost-efficient Product" and " Professional, Efficient Customer Services". 

We will work with you to integrate RFID into your current operations and help uncover areas where you may be able to leverage RFID technology.

For more info please kindly visit our website: [http://www.rfid-in-china.com](http://www.rfid-in-china.com)

### Post by NoonienSoong97 on 2009-11-20
**Bayou**

Tap: Add a swamp or a forest to your mana pool. Counts as both swamp and forest and is affected by spells that affect either. If a spell destroys one of these land types, this card is destroyed, if a spell alters one of these land types, the other land type is unaffected.

Rulings:
10/1/2008
This has the mana abilities associated with both of its basic land types. This has basic land types, but it isn't a basic land. Things that affect basic lands don't affect it. Things that affect basic land types do.

### Post by mystics on 2015-10-12
walk

Basic explanation:

```
import email

f = open("file", 'r')
msg = email.message_from_file(f)
for **part **in msg.**walk**():
#do something
```

That's some basic email stuff from Python. I believe I picked up the habit of using part...walk from reading Python's website.

### Post by jetsam on 2019-11-12
what?!

...er.

It's a game, no?  The links sometimes explain the association...  sometimes confuse the issue.

Postal--  it doesn't make sense, except in the context of the thread...  look about 5 posts back.
dogs->Who?->me->U->letters->mail->postal(posted by a 'dog')->'Who' (let the dogs out?) Both asks and answers the original "Who" with an --er --thingum...  bad pun.  I think that's what you'd call it.

### Post by QIII on 2021-06-30
Eraser

(The authorities ordered that Beethoven's body be exhumed and examined for possible foul play.  When the casket was dug up and opened, Beethoven was sitting there with a candle, music scores and a large eraser.  "Maestro!  What are you doing?" everyone cried.  Beethoven looked at them and rolled his eyes as he said "Why, decomposing, of course!")
